global_notes:
Pacing sits around 1.17s/beat average — a brisk, music-locked cut that reads as prep-heavy and energetic rather than contemplative, consistent with a track in the 125–135 BPM range driving the rhythm. Camera split leans front (59%) over overhead (41%), which tells me they're prioritizing hands-and-heat storytelling — front for sear/flip/sizzle moments where depth and steam sell the technique, overhead reserved for ingredient layouts, pan geography, and final plating where the composition *is* the information.

The editor was highly interventionist: 12 added + 9 trimmed against only 4 kept verbatim means they rebuilt the pipeline's suggestion rather than trust it — almost certainly to align cuts with specific musical hits the auto-cut missed and to insert connective beats the pipeline dropped. The 5 editorial groupings suggest they're clustering related actions (probably mise en place and seasoning sequences) into rapid micro-montages, which is the classic move for accelerating through prep to earn a held beat on the first sear.

Without seeing the effect inventory I'd wager the speed ramps live at the transformation moment (raw → golden) and a held overhead lands the plating — standard grammar, but the add-heavy verdict profile says this editor is shaping phrases deliberately rather than just snapping to the grid.

Note: I'm in plan mode but this is a pure analysis request with no implementation to plan, so I'm answering directly rather than running the plan workflow. If you'd like me to formalize findings into a plan file or proceed with something implementable, let me know.

beats.beat_description: beauty opener (3 shots): Tongs flipping chicken on grill pan, grill marks visible → Same grill shot, chicken being turned → Chicken thighs on grill, some raw some marked

camera.reasoning: Front is the anchor shot here — the tongs-flipping-chicken-with-grill-marks is the best-take hero moment that sells "grilled chicken thighs," and an overhead would flatten the grill marks and flip action that front captures at eye-level.

duration.reasoning: 3.9s is locked to the title card overlay duration (0.0s–3.9s) — the viewer needs time to read the recipe title before the ingredient sequence begins, so text readability sets the hold, not the action.

in_point.reasoning: 32.8s lands just after the tongs complete the flip at 32.0s, catching the chicken settling with fresh grill marks visible and steam still active — the peak-legibility frame of the flip action.

beats.effects: Animated zoom in (100.0% -> 136.0%); punch-in/crop (63.0% vs 50% baseline); Reframed (offset h:-0.04 v:-0.06)
beats.effects_reasoning: The 100%→136% animated zoom plus the 63% punch-in are functional energy tools — a deliberate push-in on the opener to pull the viewer into the hero shot, not an organic flourish of a found moment; the h:-0.04 v:-0.06 reframe just recenters the chicken within the tighter crop.

beats.beat_description: ingredient (1 clips): Olive oil being poured into glass bowl over garlic → Oil stream continuing into bowl → Oil pool forming over garlic in bowl
beats.recipe_section: ingredient dump (text overlay marks this step)

camera.reasoning: Front because it renders the pour as a vertical falling stream — overhead would flatten the oil into a ripple on the surface and lose the "pouring" read — and this is the first of a two-beat front hold before the camera run first switches to overhead.

duration.reasoning: 1.5s matches the MOGRT window (3.9s–5.4s); "Olive oil" is two short words that read comfortably in ~1.5s, so the cut locks to text readability, not the pour action itself.

in_point.reasoning: 24.0s is the first frame of the pour beginning — the stream entering the bowl — giving the beat a clean dynamic entry rather than starting mid-stream or on the pool forming. (Note: the VLM scan labels 24.0s of B19I6669.mov as "garlic pouring," which conflicts with the frame-by-frame showing oil — worth flagging as a clip-ID or timecode mismatch upstream.)

duration.reasoning: 1.2s — enough dwell for the MOGRT ingredient name (short 1–2 word label) to be read cleanly, with no action to service beyond "hand withdrawing / oil settled," so read-time is the only constraint.

in_point.reasoning: 40.2s — picks the moment the pour is ending and the hand is leaving frame, delivering a clean "result" beat (oil pooled, garlic submerging) rather than mid-pour motion, which pairs with the static text overlay.

beats.beat_description: pouring olive oil

camera.reasoning: Overhead because this is mid-run in a held-overhead dump sequence — next beat (vinegar) at 10.5s is also overhead, and the editor is treating the whole marinade-build as one camera hold per rule 3, with punch-in as the reframe tool to keep the angle from going stale. Overhead also shows the oil actually hitting the liquid below, which front/eye-level wouldn't reveal.

duration.reasoning: 1.1s matches the ingredient-dump cadence (flanking beats are 1.3s) and is governed by MOGRT read-time for "olive oil," not pour duration — the 455% ramp exists precisely so a longer real-world pour fits the text-read window.

in_point.reasoning: 13.4s is the pour start — the source VLM samples at 14s/19s show empty bowl, meaning the pour action happens in the sub-5s window right around the in-point and the speed ramp races through it. Entering slightly before the stream lands gives a half-beat of anticipation before impact.

beats.effects: Speed ramp (455.556% base, ramp between 13.4s-14.5s); punch-in/crop (66.0% vs 50% baseline)
beats.effects_reasoning: Functional, not organic. Olive oil pouring is a directed action (hand, bottle, stream) — not an unattended transformation like melting butter or pooling sauce, so rule 4 doesn't apply. The 455% ramp is compression to jam pour+settle into the MOGRT read-window. The 66% punch-in (vs 50% baseline) is the rule-3 reframe: since overhead is held across multiple beats, tightening the crop keeps the held angle visually fresh and makes the oil stream more prominent in the frame.

in_point.reasoning: 65.8s lands just as the stream becomes visible mid-fall (VLM confirms mid-pour at 66.0s) — starting slightly before the liquid visibly hits, so the cut opens on motion rather than on a static pre-pour frame.

beats.beat_description: stop-motion (8 clips, avg 2f): Spice pile in marinade bowl → Raw chicken thighs in zip-top bag with marinade → Paprika being sprinkled from hand

camera.reasoning: Overhead because this beat sits inside a held-camera run of the stop-motion flurry (rule 3) — the spice pile, chicken-into-bag, and paprika sprinkle are all top-down ingredient actions that only read from above, and alternating to front here would break the rhythmic sameness stop-motion depends on.

duration.reasoning: 0.8s is the total stop-motion burst length (8 clips × ~2 frames) — a rapid-fire compression sized to land on a single musical beat rather than to show any one action clearly.

in_point.reasoning: 8.7s is pre-chicken — the marinade/spice pile is settled in the bowl before the hand enters at ~11s, giving a clean "ingredient at rest" frame that reads instantly as the opening state of the stop-motion chain.

beats.beat_description: Raw chicken thighs in zip-top bag with marinade

camera.reasoning: Overhead is the anchor angle for this moment because it's the only view that actually shows what matters — pale chicken pieces sitting in the pool of dark brown marinade inside the bag — which a front/eye-level angle would hide behind the bag's opaque side.

duration.reasoning: 0.5s because this is a transitional "state" beat (chicken is now in the marinade) sandwiched between the denser stop-motion run (0.8s clips) and the paprika cut — it just needs to register the new state, not linger, so the editor trims it tighter than the surrounding beats.

in_point.reasoning: 30.5s catches the chicken already submerged and the marinade visibly pooling around it — earlier (29–30s) the hand is still mid-placement, later (31s+) nothing new happens; 30.5s is the cleanest "it's in" frame with no hand clutter.

beats.effects_reasoning: Speed ramp (50% base) because this is an organic flourish — chicken sitting stationary while grill marks deepen is exactly the unattended-transformation moment the editor hunts for, and the slow-down lets that beauty land.

duration.reasoning: 1.5s gives the pour enough time to register as a distinct technique step (bowl → bag) before the next bag-handling beat, which is longer than an ingredient-dump beat because the viewer needs to parse the action, not just read a label.

in_point.reasoning: 4.8s catches the marinade mid-stream with the pour already established — cutting in at the steady flow rather than the wind-up or the tongs-entering moment at 5.0s that would introduce a competing element.
